caveat: Figures are official data from the Office for National Statistics (ONS) and the Office for Budget Responsibility (OBR), the latest available for each measure. Public finance figures are provisional and routinely revised. Monthly borrowing is seasonal and should not be multiplied up to estimate a year.

metrics.metric_name: Public sector net debt (excluding public sector banks)
metrics.explanation: The headline measure of how much the UK government owes, after subtracting liquid assets. This is the main measure used in the monthly public finance statistics.
metrics.whatThisMeans: Think of it as everything the government has borrowed and not yet repaid, less the cash it holds. It is a stock, a total built up over many years, not a single year's spending. As a share of GDP this is around levels last seen in the early 1960s.
metrics.source_name: ONS, Public sector finances, UK: April 2026
metrics.notes: £2,917.2bn at end March 2026, the latest published cash figure. Excludes public sector banks.

metrics.metric_name: Debt as a share of GDP
metrics.explanation: Net debt measured against the size of the economy for that period. This matters more than the cash total, because a bigger economy can support more debt.
metrics.whatThisMeans: If debt is around 94% of GDP, the government owes a little less than the economy produces in a year. Economists watch the trend in this ratio rather than the cash figure alone.
metrics.source_name: ONS, Public sector finances, UK: April 2026
metrics.notes: 94.2% of GDP at end April 2026, provisionally estimated.

metrics.explanation: Total net debt divided by the UK population. A way to picture the scale, not a bill anyone personally owes.
metrics.whatThisMeans: This does not mean each person owes this amount. Government debt is repaid through taxes and borrowing over time, not split between residents.
metrics.source_name: Calculated from ONS net debt and ONS population estimate
metrics.notes: Calculated at build time: net debt (£2,917bn) divided by population estimate (69.5m). Recomputed whenever either input changes.

metrics.explanation: What the government pays to service its debt over a year. Affected both by how much is owed and by the interest rate on it.
metrics.whatThisMeans: Interest is a real cash cost competing with other spending, around 8% of total public spending. Some of it rises and falls with inflation, because a share of UK debt is index-linked. Central government interest payable was £10.3 billion in April 2026 alone.
metrics.source_name: OBR, Economic and Fiscal Outlook November 2025 (central government debt interest, net of APF)
metrics.notes: About £110bn in 2025-26 (OBR November 2025 forecast), roughly 3.6% of GDP. Includes the inflation uplift on index-linked gilts.

metrics.explanation: How much the government borrowed in the latest month, the gap between what it spent and what it raised.
metrics.whatThisMeans: Borrowing is a flow (one period's gap), while debt is the stock that builds up over many periods. Monthly borrowing is seasonal (April is typically high), so it cannot be multiplied by 12 to get the year. Annual borrowing is around £129 billion.
metrics.source_name: ONS, Public sector finances, UK: April 2026
metrics.notes: £24.3bn in April 2026, the second-highest April on record and £3.4bn above the OBR forecast of £20.9bn. Excludes public sector banks.

gdp.notes: The nominal GDP consistent with the published debt-to-GDP ratio (net debt 2917 / 0.942). Calendar-year 2025 nominal GDP outturn is about £3,037bn; this measure is used for the translator's share-of-GDP figure.

adults.notes: Population aged 18 and over. ONS has not yet published a mid-2025 age breakdown.

taxpayers.notes: Number of income tax payers (HMRC, tax year 2023-24).

taxReceipts.notes: Total tax receipts for 2025-26 (about 36% of GDP). Total public sector current receipts are higher, around £1,239bn.
